Milestone M2: scale on grounded data, axis chosen empirically

Once grounding lifts binding off the floor, push further on whichever axis the M1 rung curve indicates, generating more grounded data if binding rises with data fraction or enlarging the model if it plateaus, targeting binding at least one half and coherence at least seven.

M2 does not pre-commit to more-data versus bigger-model; it reads the lever off the M1 binding-versus-data-fraction curve (node 48). If binding rises steadily across rungs, the corpus is the constraint and the proven-but-expensive generator produces more grounded data. If binding is above floor but flat across rungs, capacity is the constraint and the preset steps up (mini to a roughly forty-million-parameter small model), reusing the grounded corpus at two to four effective epochs as the mini rung already did; current tokens per non-embedding parameter is about fifteen, near Chinchilla, so a threefold model on the same corpus stays inside the sub-four-epoch regime that held here. Gate for M2: binding exact-match at least one half and coherence at least seven on the chosen configuration.

The ordering rationale is that data precedes scale because the mini-on-ungrounded run (node 47) showed scale alone leaves binding at zero, so capacity is only worth spending once coherent data has proven it moves binding at all. The decision rule is deliberately data-driven rather than guessed: the scale-world ladder (node 43) produces nested, equal-quality corpora, so the binding score as a function of data fraction is a clean read on the marginal value of data at fixed size. A rising curve means the corpus is the binding constraint and more grounded generation is the buy, accepting that generation is the expensive step but a validated one. A flat-above-floor curve means data has saturated at this size and the return is in parameters, taken by bumping the preset while reusing the existing grounded snapshot at a few epochs, which the multi-epoch finding (node 43) shows is nearly as good as fresh data up to about four passes. The gate is set higher than M1 because M2 must produce a model good enough that the graph-context comparison (node 50) has real headroom to show a difference: binding at least one half and coherence at least seven. This milestone reads on the corpus-size parameter (node 7) and the sub-one-billion from-scratch model (node 27).
